name: Urticaria (Hives)

key_questions: Triggers: food, medication, infection, exercise, stress, cold, pressure?
New medications in past 6 weeks (especially NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, antibiotics)?
Duration of individual hives — do they last >24 hours or leave bruising/pigmentation (urticarial vasculitis)?
red_flags: Angioedema of tongue/throat or stridor → anaphylaxis/airway emergency — CALL 911
Systemic symptoms (fever, arthralgia, lymphadenopathy) → vasculitis, autoinflammatory
Chronic urticaria >6 weeks → requires investigation, refer to allergist
Individual hive lasting >24 hours or leaving bruise/pigmentation after resolving → urticarial vasculitis (refer to physician/allergist)
ddx: Urticarial vasculitis (wheals persist >24h, may leave purpuric/pigmented marks — biopsy required)

first_line.name: Non-Sedating Oral Antihistamine (2nd-generation H1)
first_line.drugs: Cetirizine (Reactine) 10 mg OD–BID | Fexofenadine (Allegra) 180 mg OD | Loratadine (Claritin) 10 mg OD | Bilastine (Blexten) 20 mg OD | Rupatadine (Rupall) 10 mg OD | Desloratadine (Aerius) 5 mg OD | Levocetirizine (Xyzal) 5 mg OD
first_line.notes: Non-sedating H1 antihistamines are the first-line for acute urticaria (EAACI/GA2LEN/WAO 2022, CSACI). Take regularly (not PRN) for better effect. Continue until urticaria fully resolved. UP-DOSING PROTOCOL (EAACI 2022): If standard dose is insufficient, increase to up to 4× the licensed dose before escalating to second-line (e.g., cetirizine 10 mg/day → 20 mg/day → 40 mg/day in divided doses; fexofenadine 180 mg → 360 mg → 720 mg/day). Try up-dosing for at least 1–2 weeks before escalating. ONTARIO MA SCOPE: Acute urticaria (<6 weeks) with non-sedating H1 antihistamines is within Ontario pharmacist MA scope (added Oct 2023). Chronic urticaria, angioedema, and prednisone are OUT of MA scope.
first_line.preferred_when: First-line for all acute urticaria; try up to 4× standard dose before escalating

second_line.name: Sedating Antihistamine (short-term add-on — use with caution)
second_line.drugs: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 25–50 mg q6h (avoid in elderly — Beers Criteria)
second_line.notes: IMPORTANT — CURRENT GUIDELINE POSITION: EAACI/GA2LEN/WAO 2022 and CSACI recommend AGAINST first-generation sedating antihistamines as routine add-on therapy for urticaria, due to significant anticholinergic burden and impaired sleep architecture. The preferred approach when standard-dose non-sedating antihistamine is insufficient is to UP-TITRATE the non-sedating agent to up to 4× the licensed dose. Use a first-generation antihistamine (e.g., diphenhydramine) only if sedation is explicitly needed for severe nocturnal pruritus interfering with sleep, and only for short-term use. Avoid in elderly (falls, confusion, cognitive impairment). Not appropriate for chronic urticaria.

second_line.name: Short-Course Prednisone (NOT in Ontario MA scope — physician referral required)
second_line.drugs: Prednisone 30–40 mg OD × 3–5 days (maximum 10 days per EAACI 2022 guideline)
second_line.notes: For severe acute urticaria not responding to antihistamine therapy including up-dosing. Do not use for chronic urticaria (no long-term benefit; significant side effects with prolonged use). Per EAACI 2022: maximum course is 10 days. ONTARIO MA SCOPE: Prednisone for urticaria is OUT of Ontario pharmacist MA scope — refer to physician.

non_pharm: Identify and avoid triggers (food diary, medication review)
Avoid ASA and NSAIDs (worsen urticaria in many patients)
refer_when: Chronic urticaria >6 weeks — refer to allergist/immunologist for investigation and omalizumab evaluation
Angioedema involvement (especially face, lips, tongue, larynx) — refer to physician; prescribe EpiPen if appropriate
Urticarial vasculitis suspected (individual wheals lasting >24h, leaving bruise or pigmentation) — requires skin biopsy and physician assessment
Requires prednisone beyond a single short course (>10 days) — out of Ontario MA scope
Suspected hereditary angioedema (no wheals, family history of recurrent swelling)
Urticaria with systemic symptoms (fever, arthralgia, lymphadenopathy) — vasculitis or autoinflammatory workup needed
Failure to respond to antihistamines including up-dosing to 4× standard dose

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Urticaria. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
CSACI (Canadian Society of Allergy and Clinical Immunology). Urticaria Management — Canadian Consensus Guidelines (2019).
Bernstein JA, et al. The diagnosis and management of acute and chronic urticaria: 2014 update. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2014;133(5):1270–1277. (AAAAI/ACAAI Joint Task Force)
Zuberbier T, et al. The EAACI/GA²LEN/EDF/WAO Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria. Allergy. 2022;77(3):734–766.
